hypotheses.reasoning_narrative: The sole positive evidence for this relationship is the mother_id field in the subject's GEDCOM record (@I467@), which points directly to @I4356@. The candidate is female, born approximately 29 years before the subject, and was still living at the time of his birth in 585 — all of which are necessary (though not sufficient) conditions for a mother-child relationship. However, the hypothesis is severely undermined by a fundamental internal contradiction: the candidate's own record does not list the subject among her children_ids, recording only @I4353@ as her child. This bidirectional inconsistency in the GEDCOM data strongly suggests either a data entry error in one or both records, or that the mother_id assignment in the subject's record was made in error. Further compounding the uncertainty, the candidate's record conflates two potentially distinct historical identities ('Oda Of Saxony' and 'Dode de Heristal Of Swabia'), meaning the record itself may be a composite of two different women, neither of whom may be the subject's actual mother. Geographic discontinuity between Normandy and Liège provides no supporting connective tissue. Given that all evidence traces to a single uncorroborated GEDCOM file and the data is internally inconsistent, confidence must remain very low despite the GEDCOM pointer existing.
alternatives_considered: The mother_id field in the subject's record may have been entered in error, and Oda/Dode may have no actual relationship to the subject.

The candidate record may represent a conflation of two historical individuals, one of whom (Dode de Heristal) is a historically attested figure associated with the Pippinid lineage — the subject's mother could be one of these two distinct women rather than the composite record.
The subject's actual mother may be an entirely unrecorded individual, with the @I4356@ assignment being a speculative or mistaken genealogical inference by the GEDCOM compiler.
Oda Of Saxony or Dode de Heristal could be a more distant maternal ancestor (grandmother or great-grandmother) rather than the direct mother, with an intervening generation omitted from the record.

issues_found: Mother record @I4356@ lists children_ids as [@I4353@] only, not including subject @I467@ — internal GEDCOM inconsistency between subject's mother_id pointer and mother's children list.
Mother's name field 'Oda Of Saxony or Dode de Heristal Of Swabia' explicitly conflates two distinct historical figures, indicating the compiler was uncertain which person this record represents.
Subject's name 'Bodilan of Burgundy' combined with birth place in Vexin/Normandy is geographically/titularly inconsistent for the era (Burgundy vs Normandy are distinct Merovingian regions).
All evidence is from a single unsourced GEDCOM; early medieval (6th-7th c.) non-royal genealogies of this period are notoriously fabricated/speculative in hobbyist files.
Mother's birth place (Heristal, Belgium) and subject's birth place (Vexin, Normandy) are ~300km apart — plausible for Merovingian aristocracy but unsupported by any marriage/migration evidence.
No Tier 1 violations, but biological plausibility alone does not establish the relationship given the structural inconsistency.
critiques.justification: The GEDCOM contains a one-sided pointer (subject→mother) without reciprocation in the mother's children list, and the mother record itself conflates two named individuals. While Tier 1 biology checks pass, the structural inconsistency and conflated identity prevent acceptance; however, asymmetric children_ids lists are common GEDCOM artifacts and do not definitively disprove the link, so outright rejection is unwarranted.
